data.abstract:
<jats:p>
            The
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            (Gagie et al., JACM 2020) represented a breakthrough in compressed indexing of repetitive text collections, outperforming its alternatives by orders of magnitude in query time. Its space usage,
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\(\mathcal{O}(r)\)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            where
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\( r \)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            is the number of runs in the Burrows–Wheeler Transform of the text, is however higher than Lempel–Ziv and grammar-based indexes and makes it uninteresting in various real-life scenarios of milder repetitiveness. In this article, we introduce the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            , a variant that limits a large fraction of the space to
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\({\mathcal{O}}(\min(r,n/s))\)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            for a text of length
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\( n \)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            and a given parameter
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\( s \)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            , at the expense of multiplying by
            <jats:inline-formula content-type="math/tex">
              <jats:tex-math notation="LaTeX" version="MathJax">\( s \)</jats:tex-math>
            </jats:inline-formula>
            the time per occurrence reported. The
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            is obtained by carefully subsampling the text positions indexed by the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            , in a way that we prove is still able to support pattern matching with guaranteed performance. Our experiments demonstrate that the theoretical analysis falls short in describing the practical advantages of the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            , because it performs much better on real texts than on synthetic ones: the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            retains the performance of the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            while using 1.5–4.0 times less space, sharply outperforming
            <jats:italic toggle="yes">virtually every other</jats:italic>
            compressed index on repetitive texts in both time and space. Only a particular Lempel–Ziv-based index uses less space—about half—than the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            , but it is an order of magnitude slower.
          </jats:p>
          <jats:p>
            Our second contribution are the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            and
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            indexes. Just like the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            adapts the well-known FM-Index to repetitive texts, the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            adapts Sadakane’s Compressed Suffix Array (CSA) to this case. We show that the principles used on the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            turn out to fit naturally and efficiently in the CSA framework. The
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            is the corresponding subsampled version of the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            . While the CSA performs better than the FM-Index on classic texts with alphabets larger than DNA, our experiments show that the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -csa
            </jats:sans-serif>
            outperforms the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">sr</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            on repetitive texts not only over those larger alphabets, but on some DNA texts as well.
          </jats:p>
          <jats:p>
            Overall, our new subsampled indexes sweep the table of the existing indexes for highly repetitive text collection, by combining the exceptional speed of the
            <jats:sans-serif>
              <jats:italic toggle="yes">r</jats:italic>
              -index
            </jats:sans-serif>
            with drastically reduced storage use.
          </jats:p>
data.title: Fast and Small Subsampled R-indexes
